macro_segments.title: The Debate Between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva: Specificity of Knowledge Regarding Tumah

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Final Section of Perek One: Tumat Mikdash and Korban Oleh V'Yored
The Two Cases That Become Four: Forgetting Tumah vs. Forgetting Mikdash
Rabbi Akiva's Position: Only Helam Tumah Obligates a Korban
Rabbi Yishmael's Dispute: Both Helam Tumah and Helam Mikdash Obligate
The Opening Mishnah: Rabbi Eliezer's Statement on Helam Sheretz
Gemara's Question: What is the Difference Between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva?

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva: Specificity of the Source of Tumah

micro_segments.title: Chizkiyya's Explanation: The Debate Concerns Knowledge of How One Became Tamei
Rabbi Eliezer's Requirement: Concrete Knowledge of the Specific Source of Tumah
Rabbi Akiva's Disagreement: General Knowledge of Tumah Suffices
Ulla's Explanation: The Difference is Based on Specificity of Biblical Language

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's General Principle: Uncertainty About the Type of Sin Does Not Exempt

micro_segments.title: The Case of Cheilev and Nosar: Does One Need to Know Which Forbidden Food Was Eaten?
Rabbi Eliezer's Response: Mah Nafshach - the Outcome is the Same Either Way
Analogous Cases: Shabbat vs. Yom Kippur, Niddah vs. Sister
Rabbi Yehoshua's Objection: The Verse Requires Knowing the Specific Sin

macro_segments.title: Resolution of Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua: Different Frameworks for Korban Chatat

micro_segments.title: Ulla's Answer: The Focus of Asher Chata Bah is on the Sin, Not Specificity
Distinction Between General Korban Chatat and Tumat Mikdash Framework

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva: Specificity of the Source of Tumah (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Role of Pshat in the Pesukim: Why Torah Specifies Sheretz and Neveilah

macro_segments.title: Resolution of Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua: Different Frameworks for Korban Chatat (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Specificity of Tumat Mikdash: High Level of Discernment Required

macro_segments.title: Reframing the Debate: Rabi Yochanan's Position on Practical Disagreement vs. Source Difference

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan: The Debate is Only About Which Part of the Pesukim is the Source
Rav Sheshet's Variation: Sometimes Reversing the Names of Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva

macro_segments.title: Practical Question: When Both Tumah and Mikdash Are Forgotten

micro_segments.title: Rava's Question: If Both Tumah and Mikdash Are Forgotten, Is a Korban Brought?
First Answer: If You Forgot Tumah, You Bring a Korban
Alternative Answer: If You Forgot Mikdash, You Are Exempt
Rav Ashi's Test: Which Awareness Would Have Triggered Memory of the Other?
Ravina's Objection: Both Were Forgotten, So Both Determinations Seem Equal
Rashi's Resolution: The Conclusion is That One is Exempt (Patur)

macro_segments.title: The Two Paths Scenario: Uncertainty About Which Path Led to Tumah

micro_segments.title: Case One: Both Paths Traversed, Then Entered Mikdash - Definitely Obligated
Case Two: First Path, Then Mikdash, Purified, Then Second Path, Then Mikdash - Chiyuv
Rabbi Shimon's Position: Exempt Even in the First Case
Gemara's Challenge: Why Would Rabbi Shimon Exempt if Definitely Tamei?
Rava's Resolution: The Case Involves Only Partial Awareness of Definite Tumah
The Debate: Is Partial Knowledge of Definite Tumah Sufficient (Tanna Kamma) or Not (Rabbi Shimon)?

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Investigation: Is Safek Yedia Comparable to Full Yedia in Tumat Mikdash?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan: Safek Yedia Counts as Full Yedia in Tumat Mikdash (Exception)
Reish Lakish: Attributes the Position to Rabbi Yishmael, Who Doesn't Require Initial Awareness

macro_segments.title: Parallel Case: Safek Cheilev on Separate Days and the Question of Multiple Korbanot

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: One Possibly Cheilev, Then Aware, Another Possibly Cheilev, Then Aware
Rabbi's Opinion: Two Separate Asham Talui Offerings for Two Doubtful Awarenesses
Rabbi Shimon ben Yehuda: Only One Asham Talui for All Doubtful Sins of the Same Type
Reish Lakish's Reading: Doubtful Awareness (Yedi'ot Sfekot) Divides into Multiple Korbanot
Rabbi Yochanan's Counter-Reading: Only Definite Awareness Divides, Doubtful Divides into Asham Talui

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Investigation: Is Safek Yedia Comparable to Full Yedia in Tumat Mikdash? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Reasoning: Tumat Mikdash Derives Awareness from V'Ne'elam, Not Explicit Yida

macro_segments.title: Reconciling the Contradiction: Why Safek Yedia Works in Tumat Mikdash But Not Elsewhere

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Point: Tumat Mikdash is an Exception Where Safek Yedia Suffices
Reason: Torah Derives Awareness from V'Ne'elam Without Explicit Yida Language
Contrast: Other Korbanot Require Full Awareness (O Hoda Eilav)
Rav Ashi's Conclusion: The Weaker Inference Allows for Weaker Awareness (Safek)
Reish Lakish's Alternative: Attribute to Rabbi Yishmael's View of No Initial Awareness Requirement
Gemara's Assertion: Reish Lakish Underscores That Rabbi Yishmael Never Required Initial Awareness

macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek Three: Shebuot - Oaths and Their Violations

micro_segments.title: Transition from Tumat Mikdash to the Main Topic of Shebuot (Oaths)
Two Cases That Become Four: Future-Oriented vs. Past-Oriented Oaths
Torah's Focus: Doing Good or Bad (Lehara O Lehaitiv) in Future Actions
The Four Cases: Shebuot About Eating/Not Eating (Future) and Past Claims

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's Position: Violating an Oath Requires Only Minimal Amount

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Oath Not to Eat, Then Eating Any Amount Obligates a Korban
Rabbi Akiva's Reasoning: Speech Acts Differ from Ordinary Aveirot - Intent Determines Amount
Rashi's Explanation: The Issue is Bitul Divur (Negating Your Word), Not Physical Act
Distinction: Unlike Neveilah (Fixed Kezayit), An Oath's Amount Depends on Intent
